old_claim.note: Microprocessors (CPU on a Chip) provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: RISC-I was a single-chip VLSI processor in a lineage after earlier CPU-on-a-chip microprocessors; the prior microprocessor category is historical substrate, not the distinctive RISC method.

source_shape.source_locator: IEEE ETHW calls RISC-I the first VLSI RISC microprocessor and describes its single-chip processor implementation after earlier processor-chip technology existed.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: RISC-I belongs in the microprocessor lineage but is distinguished by reduced-instruction-set VLSI design choices.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports a historical predecessor edge because the broad microprocessor category precedes the scoped RISC implementation but does not itself define the RISC architectural method.

ontology_before: The graph treated earlier microprocessors as a generic enabling dependency.
ontology_after: The graph treats earlier CPU-on-chip microprocessors as lineage while VLSI design carries the direct implementation requirement.
would_reject_if: The node were rescoped to abstract RISC architecture rather than RISC microprocessors.
A stronger source showed CPU-on-chip microprocessors were a strict component prerequisite rather than a lineage/category predecessor.
short_reason: Earlier microprocessors are lineage, not the distinctive RISC implementation method.
